title: Tax Basics for Non-Tax Accountants
subhead: You don't need to be a tax accountant. You need to not make their job harder — and not trigger the penalties that fall on the staff accountant who got the deposit timing or 1099 threshold wrong.
hook: "Why did we get an IRS penalty notice?" The answer, in most small companies, is that a staff accountant didn't deposit payroll taxes on time, didn't file a 1099 for a contractor over $600, or treated sales tax as revenue. These are the six things you will get wrong in year one if you don't learn them here.

chunks.title: The Taxes Every Staff Accountant Encounters
chunks.bodyHtml:
What this module is NOT: a comprehensive tax guide. Tax changes constantly and needs a specialist. What it IS: the 6 tax concepts you WILL encounter in the first year that you must handle correctly.

1. Payroll Tax Deposits

FICA (Social Security + Medicare) must be deposited with the IRS on a semi-weekly or monthly schedule depending on payroll size. The deposit is separate from the payroll journal entry — you record the liability in the JE, then deposit it later. Late deposits trigger a 2–15% penalty — the #1 tax penalty entry-level accountants accidentally cause.

Skarion context: monthly deposit of $18,312 (employer FICA $6,256 + employee FICA withheld ≈ $12,056) via EFTPS by the 15th of the following month.

2. Sales Tax (product-selling companies)

Collected from customers at point of sale, remitted to the state monthly/quarterly. It's NOT revenue — it's a liability (Sales Tax Payable) from the moment it's collected. Skarion is a services firm so doesn't collect sales tax on designs — but if it sold physical cables/equipment it would.

JE when selling taxable goods: Dr Cash $106,000 / Cr Revenue $100,000 / Cr Sales Tax Payable $6,000.

3. Use Tax

When you buy taxable goods from an out-of-state vendor who doesn't charge sales tax, you owe use tax to your state. Most companies ignore this until an audit — accountants should track it.

Skarion context: $12,000 of survey equipment bought from an Ohio vendor with no Virginia sales tax → Virginia use tax $636 ($12,000 × 5.3%). Book Dr Equipment $12,636 / Cr Cash $12,636 (or split the use tax to a Use Tax Payable liability to remit later).

4. 1099 Filing

Any contractor paid more than $600 in a year must receive a 1099-NEC by January 31. The accountant tracks all contractor payments and prepares 1099s at year-end.

Skarion context: Global CAD Solutions receives ~$600,000/year — they get a 1099-NEC annually. Miss it and the company can be liable for the contractor's tax.

5. W-2 Preparation

All W2 employees receive their W-2 by January 31. The data comes from the payroll records you maintained all year. The W-2 totals must match the payroll tax deposits exactly — discrepancies trigger IRS notices.

6. Estimated Tax Payments (for pass-throughs)

If Skarion is an LLC or S-Corp, the owner pays quarterly estimated taxes on their share of profit. The accountant prepares the calc: prior year's tax liability ÷ 4, paid in April/June/September/January. Often confused with the business's payroll taxes — they are completely different.

chunks.title: 6 Tax Scenarios — The Right Treatment and the Penalty for Getting It Wrong

cards.back: FICA + withholdings deposited to the IRS per your schedule (monthly for Skarion: ~$18,312 via EFTPS by the 15th). Recorded as a liability in the payroll JE, then remitted in a separate JE (Dr Payroll Tax Liabilities / Cr Cash). Late = 2–15% penalty.

cards.front: Sales tax on a taxable sale
cards.back: Sales tax is collected as a liability, never as revenue. JE: Dr Cash $106,000 / Cr Revenue $100,000 / Cr Sales Tax Payable $6,000. Remit to the state; never spend it as if it were yours. Skarion (services) doesn't collect sales tax on designs.

cards.front: Use tax on an out-of-state purchase
cards.back: No sales tax charged by the out-of-state vendor → you self-assess use tax to your state. Skarion's $12,000 Ohio equipment = $636 Virginia use tax. Track it on a Use Tax Payable liability; most companies skip this until audit catches it.

Any 1099 contractor paid ≥ $600/year gets a 1099-NEC by January 31. Global CAD Solutions (~$600K/year) gets one. Failure penalizes the company — track contractor payments all year and verify each vendor's W-9.

All W2 employees get a W-2 by Jan 31. The W-2 totals MUST equal the payroll tax deposits for the year — a $1 discrepancy triggers an IRS notice. Data integrity from the payroll records you maintained all year is the whole game.

Pass-through owners (LLC/S-Corp) pay quarterly estimates — prior year tax ÷ 4, due April/June/September/January. Different from the company's payroll tax deposits. You prepare the calc; the owner pays it personally.

chunks.title: Tax vs. Book Differences — Why Two Sets of Numbers Exist
chunks.bodyHtml:
The concept most self-taught accountants never learn — and most interviews touch on.

Book accounting follows GAAP — for investors, banks, and management. Tax accounting follows IRS rules — for the tax return. These two are different. That's legal, expected, and managed through deferred tax assets and liabilities.

The most common differences:

  1. Depreciation: GAAP uses straight-line. The IRS often allows accelerated (MACRS) or immediate expensing (Section 179). Same asset, different annual expense for book vs tax.
  2. Revenue recognition: GAAP recognizes on delivery (ASC 606). Tax sometimes allows the installment method — recognize revenue as cash is received.
  3. Bad debt: GAAP uses the allowance method (estimate and reserve). Tax only allows write-off when the debt is actually uncollectible (the specific charge-off method).
  4. Meals & entertainment: 100% book expense; only 50% deductible for tax (0% for entertainment post-2017 TCJA).

The key concept — deferred tax: when GAAP records more income than the tax return (e.g., GAAP straight-line depreciation is slower than tax accelerated), the company will pay that tax later → a Deferred Tax Liability (DTL). When GAAP records less income than tax (e.g., tax accelerated depreciation front-loads expense), the company has prepaid tax it'll use later → a Deferred Tax Asset (DTA).

chunks.title: Book vs. Tax — 3 Real Scenarios
chunks.intro: Apply the book/tax difference to real Skarion decisions. The deferred tax concept gets tested in every accounting interview that goes above bookkeeping.

questions.q: Skarion depreciates a $70,000 server over 7 years straight-line ($10,000/yr GAAP). For tax, they elect 100% bonus depreciation in Year 1. At a 21% rate, what deferred tax liability is created in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Year 1: GAAP books $10,000 depreciation expense; tax books $70,000. So GAAP income is $60,000 higher than taxable income. Temporary difference $60,000 × 21% = $12,600 DTL. (The closest listed is the styled answer; precise figure from $60K × 21%.) A DTL — you'll pay that tax later as the depreciation reverses.

questions.q: Skarion writes off TeleCom Builders' $112,700 AR under GAAP's allowance method in Q3. For tax, the deduction is only allowed when the debt is legally uncollectible in Q4. When does the deferred tax ASSET reverse?
options: In Q4, when the tax deduction becomes allowable (book and tax converge)
questions.explanation: Book took the expense in Q3 (allowance method), tax takes it in Q4 (specific charge-off). The DTA created in Q3 reverses in Q4 when the tax deduction lands — a temporary difference, not permanent.

questions.q: Skarion takes the team to dinner — $840 restaurant bill. How much is deductible for tax?
questions.explanation: Meals (business, substantiated) are 50% deductible for tax; 100% is the book expense. Entertainment (post-2017 TCJA) is 0% deductible. The $420 difference creates a small permanent — not deferred — book/tax difference.
